data.title: FLBR: Still Cheap, But Not As Compelling As Before (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

The Franklin FTSE Brazil ETF (NYSEARCA:FLBR), which has been around for close to eight years and passively tracks the FTSE Brazil Capped Index, represents the most cost-efficient ETF vehicle (an expense ratio of just 0.19%, which is the gold standard among all Brazilian ETFs) for those looking to gain access to a pool of Brazilian stocks.

FLBR, which currently consists of 71 Brazilian stocks (around half of which are giant caps), was last covered by us in June 2024, when it wasn’t having its finest hour and underperforming EMs and global markets by a large margin; yet, we chose to take a contrarian stance then by positing a buy rating on the ETF.

Our buy stance then was driven by a favorable macro picture, still healthy yields, cheap valuations, and good risk-reward on the charts.

FLBR has since embarked on a much better run, generating positive returns of +22%, although admittedly, it has still not quite been able to trump the performance of global stocks and EMs, which have ended up outperforming by a few percentage points more.

We’ll take what we get, but given that it’s been around 15 months since our last coverage, we felt an update was long overdue.

Economic Backdrop Isn’t The Most Resilient

Firstly, it’s fair to say that Brazil’s macroeconomic backdrop isn’t in the best of health. Q2 real GDP growth of just 2.2% was the worst in over three years, with the Brazilian central bank’s high policy rate (15%) and +5% inflation readings for multiple months since February (whereas the monetary council’s target is a lot lower at 3%) having prevented the economy from flourishing at full tilt.

Even more recently, the IBC-Br economic activity index, a more expeditious measure of GDP (this comes out every month), has been on a downward slump for three months, with July too witnessing a -0.5% decline!

It is questionable if there will be a bounce any time soon (FY25 GDP growth expectations are quite weak at only 2.3%, down from previous expectations of 2.5%), as the leading economic index [LEI] provided by the Conference Board has been on a declining trend, with August’s decline of -0.9% coming in worse than what was seen in July (-0.3%).

High interest rates and an adverse growth environment will also reflect poorly on the growth prospects of the loan book of Brazilian banks, which account for 31% of FLBR’s portfolio (twice as much as the next largest sector).

As you can see, all through this year, loan growth in Brazil has been coming in at less than 1%, with the most recent reading representing the worst performance in 6 months!

Note that Brazil's commodity exports too have been badly hit by US tariffs (dropping by double-digit terms in August), but there is also some scope that some rapprochement may be seen going forward, given faint positive discussions between Lula and Trump at the UN General Discussions earlier this week.

The Dividend Facet Is Not So Bright Anymore

The last time we wrote about FLBR, we talked up its above-average yield of 7.3%. However, at current prices, one only gets a yield closer to 5%, which is almost 250 bps lower than the stock’s long-term average. It isn’t just the superior price performance that has left an adverse mark on the yields; FLBR’s last two semi-annual distributions have declined by -11% YoY and -44% YoY, respectively!

This is a reflection of Brazilian companies choosing to be less generous with their payouts; for instance, during H1 2025, Brazilian listed companies cut their dividend payouts by 6% YoY, while the top 10 payers (who contributed 42% of total payouts) cut their payouts by an even larger margin of 14% YoY!

Going forward, there are some suggestions that FLBR’s top holding, Vale S.A. (VALE), may choose to be more generous with its distributions in H2, particularly on account of its recent decision to trim its CAPEX budget. However, FLBR holders should also be aware that currently there is a tax reform proposal that could see the onset of a 10% withholding income tax on dividends paid to non-resident shareholders. This is still up for debate, but if levied, it will come through by the turn of 2026 and could result in even lower distributions by FLBR (this may also prompt VALE to rather pursue higher share buybacks than be overly generous with the dividends).

Valuations Are Still Cheap

An overriding reason to still believe in the Brazilian story is that the valuations here still remain quite conservative (although not as cheap as what they were during our previous coverage) relative to other territories across the globe.

FLBR is priced at a forward P/E of just 8.3x, which works out to a discount of 40-60% over EMs and global stocks. However, crucially, at that lowly multiple, its holdings are still set to deliver superior long-term earnings growth of double digits, not something EMs or global markets can boast of.

Given this favorable differential, it is questionable if FLBR’s below-average relative strength ratio (vs. emerging markets) is warranted.

Solid Momentum May Ebb As Signs Of Intermediate Overextension Pick Up

Momentum players may be basking in FLBR’s trend of witnessing a series of higher highs (HHs) and higher lows (HLs) since around March, while the ETF also trades above all its key moving averages. Also note that the last 8 weekly candles have all been green-bodied candles, implying that the close of the week was higher than the open of the week. All these are classic momentum traits.

However, we now have reason to believe that a pause may be around the corner. Note that on the FLBR’s weekly chart, the candles over the last two weeks have been characterized by upper wicks (reflecting buying fatigue), while the RSI indicator (at the lower end of the chart) is already at its highest point in 3.5 years and is not far from hitting overbought levels. The last time FLBR hit overbought levels was in March 2022, after which there was a ~25% price correction. We are not necessarily suggesting that there will be a repeated price correction of this magnitude, but questions may certainly be asked about the price action being overextended on an intermediate basis. Also consider that FLBR is currently closing outside its upper Bollinger Band (a measure of overextension since it is 2 standard deviations higher than the 20-week rolling average), and history has shown that this is generally not sustainable.

Closing Thoughts - Rating Revision

While FLBR still has quite a few supportive tailwinds (cheap valuations, strong momentum), the investment case isn’t as robust as it was 15 months back (macros look unsteady, and the dividend picture isn't as impressive), and thus we are choosing to shift from a buy to a hold.

data.title: Freshworks: A Maturing SaaS Story With Growing Cash Flows
data.description:
Freshworks Inc. (NASDAQ:FRSH) has carved out a niche in the software-as-a-service (SaaS) market with products designed to make customer and employee experiences simpler and more affordable than legacy platforms.

The company is growing to scale. While high revenue growth rates have started to decline, profits and cash flows have expanded. With management heavily emphasizing artificial intelligence through its “Freddy” AI platform and a sharper focus on mid-market and enterprise accounts, Freshworks is showing investors that it can evolve from a high-growth story into a more balanced growth-and-profitability play.

The challenge is weighing whether Freshworks’ moderating revenue trajectory can still support upside. Competitors like Salesforce (CRM), ServiceNow (NOW), and HubSpot (HUBS) are investing aggressively in similar AI-driven solutions, and the company must prove its differentiators are sustainable. The combination of improving margins, sticky customers, and a growing product portfolio keeps the stock interesting.

Company Overview

Freshworks was founded in 2010 and today operates from San Mateo, California, though it retains deep roots in India, where much of its R&D is located. The company’s software suite is aimed at streamlining customer experience (CX) and employee support (EX). Its flagship products include Freshdesk (help desk and customer support), Freshsales (CRM and sales automation), Freshchat (messaging), and Freshservice (IT service management). Customers pay a subscription fee for access to the software.

Freshworks differentiates itself with ease of deployment and lower complexity compared to incumbents. A smaller business can get up and running with Freshworks far faster than with Salesforce Service Cloud, for example, while paying less. In recent years, the company has made acquisitions—most notably Device42 in 2024—to expand its IT asset management offerings and deepen its enterprise service management capabilities.

The customer base is broad, spanning SMBs, mid-market companies, and increasingly enterprise customers. No single customer accounts for more than 1% of revenue, which reduces concentration risk. Geographically, the company generates about 42% of revenue from North America, 38% from EMEA, and the rest from APAC and other regions.

Financial Review

Freshworks’ recent results highlight the transition from high growth with heavy losses to more measured growth with improving profitability.

The company has persistently been a loss-making entity. As a SaaS company, the important factor is scale to achieve operating leverage and become profitable. The cost to bring a new customer onto the platform is minimal. Financial data taken from company 10-Q. Q2 2025 revenue came in at $204.7 million, up 18% year-over-year, slightly above consensus expectations. Operating leverage is now beginning to show; non-GAAP operating income rose to $44.8 million, up from just $13.1 million a year ago. Margins are improving across the board. Gross margin improved to a very impressive 84.8%, up 1 percentage point from the prior year. The non-GAAP operating margin increased to 22%, up 14 percentage points.

GAAP results remain negative, with a small operating loss of $8.7 million, but this is a dramatic improvement from the prior year when the quarterly loss topped $40 million. The company has very high costs on the G&A and Sales and Marketing. Much of this is due to stock-based comp. I have gone into more detail on the stock-based comp in its own section below. Even after backing out the stock-based comp the company still has very high Sales and Marketing and G&A costs. I understand that without sales the engine does not keep moving; also, the company is in a growth stage. That being said, the high levels of operating costs are concerning. Between Sales and Marketing and G&A, the expense was over 50% of revenues. That makes it hard to generate a lot of profit. So while the company is beginning to see operating leverage, it still has a way to go on the expense side. The company notes in their filings that they expect Sales and Marketing and G&A costs to continue to increase but at a slower rate than sales, causing their percentage of revenue to continue to drop. This trend has already been happening for the company. The table below outlines the percentage of revenue coming from each line item. Note that these do include stock-based comp.

Cash flow is a strong point for the company. Stock-based comp is what is driving much of the losses, and that is not a cash item. The company had strong free cash flow of $54.3 million, which translated to a margin of nearly 27%. This, added with the sales growth of 18%, allows the company to achieve the Rule of 40.

The balance sheet is robust with $926 million in cash and marketable securities as of the end of Q2 2025, giving the company flexibility for acquisitions and buybacks. Freshworks has already repurchased approximately $240 million worth of stock under a $400 million authorization.

Management noted during the conference call that customers with more than $5,000 in ARR grew 10% to nearly 24,000, and net dollar retention is holding at 106%. These are good, not spectacular, figures that underscore the durability of the base but also hint at limited upside expansion without new products or deeper penetration.

Stock-Based Comp

A big item for the company is stock-based compensation, or SBC. This is what drives the difference between the GAAP vs. non-GAAP results. For example, more than half of the G&A expense for Q2 2025 is SBC. Once that SBC is backed out, the profitability picture is very different.

As a smaller software company, there is some SBC to be expected, but Freshworks has had very high levels of SBC over the past few years of being a public company. The company had $207 million, $210 million, and $216 million in stock-based comp in 2022, 2023, and 2024, respectively. This was equivalent to 41.7%, 35.3%, and 30.1% of revenues in those same years. This number is a very large percentage, but as revenues continue to grow, the SBC as a percentage of revenue continues to get smaller. In Q2 the company had $50 million in stock-based comp making up 24.6% of revenues.

The SBC is still much higher than I would like to see. I understand the nature of it, and the actual dollar amount is shrinking, not just the percentage of revenues. The estimated SBC going forward is outlined in the company filings. The company has $386.6 million in unrecognized SBC at the end of Q2 2025. Some of this is dependent upon targets being achieved as well, so not all of it might materialize. This is expected to be expensed over the next 2.9 years.

Based upon the data from the company, the SBC over the next 3 years will be much smaller than what has been seen in the prior years. 2025 is already seeing a decline in the actual amount, as SBC over the first 6 months was $102 million vs. $108 million in the prior year. I would expect to see the SBC continue to decline as the company is further removed from its IPO and as it matures.

While SBC is not a direct cash cost for a company, it is a form of dilution to shareholders. All of the gains can quickly be eaten up by new shares being issued. While dilution is commonly the case, it is not the case with Freshworks. While stock-based comp has been extremely high, the share count only increased by 3% in 2023 and 2.6% in 2024. The share count at the end of Q2 2025 was lower than the prior year. The company has used their cash to buy back stock and keep dilution in check. The company still has $159 million remaining on its buyback plan, which is 4.2% of the float. The remaining $386.6 million in SBC to be realized over the next 3 years is approximately 10% of the float. On top of buying back stock through the share repurchase plan, the management also noted that they “continue to manage and offset share count dilution by net settlement invested equity amounts.” They used $14 million in Q2 for that purpose. These amounts are excluded from the free cash flow calculation as well. For the full year, the company expects to use $64 million to net settle vested equity accounts. Between the buyback and net settlement of equity accounts, management has estimated that there will be 296.9 million shares outstanding at year end, a 1.3% drop in share count.

Freshworks is not in the same position as many startup companies. It is already generating strong cash flow. It has been using some of that cash flow to keep dilution in check. This is great for shareholders. The only concern is that they could be using that cash elsewhere in the business. Particularly in product development to assure they are keeping up with competition. I think the stock repurchases are a sign of the company maturing out of the start-up stage.

Outlook

Management’s guidance reflects slowing growth and also margin compression. For Q3 2025, revenue is projected to be between $207 and $210 million, representing 11–12% growth. Non-GAAP income from operations is expected to be $31.2-33.2 million. This is a pretty good drop-off from the $44.8 million in Q2. Non-GAAP net income per share is also expected to drop from $0.18 in Q2 to $0.14 in Q3.

For the full year, guidance is $823–829 million, equaling 14–15% growth. Non-GAAP operating income is expected to be in the $153–157 million range, and non-GAAP net income per share is expected to be $0.58 for the year. Once again, these numbers estimate a slowdown in operating income and profit in Q3 and Q4 as compared to the first half of the year. There appears to be some expected margin compression for the second half of the year. The company does regularly beat estimates, so these estimates are probably a bit low. Regardless, management is guiding for lower profitability, as I would expect profitability to be increasing as it continues to benefit from operating leverage.

The big talking point on the outlook is AI. The company, like its competitors, has been investing in AI to improve the software and automate processes for customers. The company has rolled out its Freddy AI products to both sides of the business, CX and EX. This will continue to be a big talking point and important factor in regard to the success of the platform. The company needs Freddy AI to be efficient and provide value to the customer. If not, then the company could get lapped by competitors who are also investing in AI or their platforms. Early signs show a lot of success; management noted that Freddy Copilot was included in more than 55% of the new large customer deals. The company launched Freddy AI Agent in June. This can be used in a variety of functions. Lastly, the company launched Freddy AI Insights in Q2. Many of these products are new on the scene for the company. I expect these will continue to gain steam in 2025 and to be a larger driver for revenue in 2026.

Overall, the company’s growth strategy centers on three pillars:

  1. AI Expansion – Discussed a bit more above, the Freddy AI suite is being cross-sold into both CX and EX products. Early adoption has been promising. The company saw $20 million in ARR from its AI. This ARR had a sequential growth rate of 21% from Q1 to Q2. While these are great signs at $20 million, it is not yet a large revenue contributor.

  2. Enterprise Focus – By targeting larger accounts, Freshworks can boost average ARR per customer, even if total customer growth moderates.

  3. Profit Discipline – Stock repurchases, cost control, and careful investment in R&D are aimed at improving free cash flow margins further.

Overall, it appears that growth is slowing from the 20%-plus rate previously experienced. A mid-teens growth rate is still a solid growth rate. If the company can continue to operate efficiently, then margins and profits should continue to grow. This profile makes Freshworks look less like a hyper-growth SaaS company and more like a maturing mid-cap with consistent cash generation.

Risks

Competition is intense within the industry. You have competitors such as Salesforce, ServiceNow, HubSpot, and others that are embedding AI into their platforms. Freshworks must prove that its simpler, lower-cost positioning resonates enough to withstand competitive pricing or feature wars. It must continue to innovate its product to compete with some of these much larger players. That takes cash to invest back into the business.

The company is seeing the revenue growth rate decline. The shift from 20%+ growth to low- to mid-teens could pressure valuation multiples if investors view the company as “ex-growth.” I think there is still room to grow, particularly through its AI investments.

Non-GAAP profits look strong, but SBC remains a major expense, leaving GAAP results negative. While it appears that SBC is beginning to decline, the company has set aside large amounts of SBC to potentially be used in the future. The company has done a good job of mitigating dilution. This reduces the amount of cash available to invest back into the business. If the company continues to issue massive amounts of SBC, it could dampen any potential returns to investors.

The company is heavily investing in AI. Freddy’s success depends on customer adoption, quality of outputs, and integration with workflows. Missteps could damage credibility.

Valuation and Stock Performance

Freshworks had a massive IPO, valuing the company at $10.13 billion in 2021. The price hit highs above $50 in the fall of 2021. The stock quickly fell from there, with the price falling under $12 in less than a year. It has ebbed and flowed over the past few years. In the past year, the stock hit a high of $19.77 back in January. The stock has been on a downward trend as of the last few months; the stock is down 19.5% over the past 6 months and down 5.6% in the last month alone. It currently sits at a market cap of approximately $3.8 billion. Down a significant amount from its IPO pricing.

At the current price, Freshworks seems undervalued when compared to the competition. While it is not always best to compare companies that are of much different market cap, the most direct competitors to Freshworks are quite a bit larger. There are other companies competing, such as Microsoft, but their business is far more sprawling than the competitors chosen to compare. These companies are directly competing with Freshworks on either the CX, EX, or both sides of the business. The table below outlines the comparison between Freshworks, ServiceNow, Inc., HubSpot, Inc., Salesforce, Inc., and Atlassian Corporation (TEAM).

Freshworks is undervalued on all the valuation and growth metrics. While the company is undervalued by nearly 50% on the PE ratio, the revenue growth is on par with the competitors. Based upon a comp analysis, Freshworks is undervalued.

The concerns that might cause a lower valuation are around GAAP profitability and scale. Freshworks is not GAAP profitable. It also does not have a history of churning out profits. I think that is more due to its timeframe of being a public company and time for the SBC to flush through the system. The company is also not at scale to better use operating leverage for its software. I see it not being at scale as an advantage; it means the company has a lot of room to grow and generate higher profits. The other concern is size and scale. These larger competitors have a lot more resources than Freshworks to build their AI systems and advance their product portfolio. The other thing to consider is that being smaller allows Freshworks to better operate in the small- to medium-size business sector.

The market seems to be pricing Freshworks as a company that might have a hard time competing profitably with some of the bigger players in the industry. If the company can sustain mid-teens revenue growth while improving margins, the current valuation looks low.

Conclusion

Freshworks is a company that is shifting from a high-growth SaaS newcomer to a disciplined operator balancing growth with profitability. Revenue growth is moderating, but margins have been improving sharply, free cash flow is robust, and the balance sheet is strong. The only concern I have is that the company has forecasted lower margins in the second half of 2025. As the company scales, I would expect margins to grow. Management noted some investments in the second half of the year that might reduce margins a bit. AI remains the wildcard; if Freshworks can prove its Freddy suite is more than a buzzword and actually monetizes at scale, the company could justify a premium valuation.

The company is currently undervalued compared to competitors in the market. I think the slowing sales have investors concerned, along with the lack of GAAP profitability. Also, the size/resources of Freshworks as compared with the competition going into an AI arms race seem to have Freshworks trading at discounted multiples.

Freshworks is in a competitive market, but it is showing that it has found a place in that market. Growth is slowing but still expected to continue in the mid-teens going forward. The company is achieving scale, and along with that, profits and cash flow. Compared with competitors, it is trading at a discount. Trading at the lower end of the 52-week price range, I think Freshworks has room to run and rate it a buy.

data.title: Pfizer: Buy The Pharma Giant Following The Metsera Deal
data.description:
Introduction

I’ve covered Pfizer Inc. (NYSE:PFE) quite extensively over the past couple of months, and I wanted to dive into the recent acquisition that the legacy pharmaceutical company has just made to see whether it is a good deal or not for the company.

Pfizer has remained slightly flat since my last analysis, as it shed the gains it made following its strong earnings result. With the deal just inked, I will delve into how this may impact the turnaround story.

The Deal

Pfizer announced on Monday that it will buy the weight loss drug biotech Metsera in a deal that could reach up to $7.3B. As to the structure of the deal, Pfizer will pay a starting $47.5 per share, which consists of a premium of around 43% to the company’s last closing price. There is also a further contingent payment of up to $22.5 a share on potential clinical and regulatory achievements, which would bring the total value of the deal to $70 a share.

This marks a drastic shift for Pfizer as it tries to compete in the high-growth area in the pharmaceutical industry that is the obesity treatment landscape. It plans on competing with the top dogs in the space, which are Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) and Eli Lilly and Company (LLY), and it comes after Pfizer has faced a couple of setbacks in the obesity race. Pfizer had to end the development of its own obesity drug, danuglipron, due to apparent safety concerns. Furthermore, Pfizer also scrapped another once-daily pill back in 2023, mainly due to elevated liver enzymes. There were other earlier-stage obesity drugs in the pipeline, but the company has been slow to roll them, and frankly, I’ll take the acquisition with a better track record in the space.

Metsera, Inc. (MTSR), which is a relatively young company, has a strong pipeline that consists of both oral and injectable solutions, including MET-233i, which has demonstrated in its early-stage trials that it could help patients lose close to 8.4% of their weight in 36 days. This could become a monthly injection rather than the current weekly ones on the market, providing a slight advantage in terms of convenience. Furthermore, Metsera is also working on two oral GLP-1 candidates that are set to begin the trial phase. This is coupled with current research for treatments based on the hormone amylin.

Pfizer has highlighted how huge the opportunity could be, as the weight loss drug space is estimated to be worth up to $150B by 2030, leaving room to compete with the two other giants. Furthermore, Pfizer will be able to use its manufacturing might as well as its commercial potential to accelerate the growth story of Metsera. This could be a tremendous opportunity for Pfizer, especially as the major problem for the legacy pharma company has been the drug pipeline that has crumbled following the massive R&D spending made during the pandemic period. Plus, entering the high-growth segment could be a huge revenue driver, in which Pfizer has seen a massive deceleration since 2021, as the COVID-19 revenue fell off.

Valuation

Pfizer is still massively undervalued in my opinion, with key valuation metrics being both at the lower end of the industry as well as decades low for Pfizer. The company currently has a P/E Non-GAAP of 7.09 and an EV/EBITDA of 10.8. Another highlight of the downside in share price would be the current FCF yield that investors can now have, which stands at around 9.1%, higher than companies such as Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) and Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY). Another big purchasing advantage I pushed forward for investors was the current dividend yield, standing at 7.11%. This is certainly a great opportunity for investors to wait for the company to see a turnaround. Considering the recent deal, I believe that this could be a strong catalyst for Pfizer to see its valuation multiples increase as it can now access a higher-growth market, which could prove to be a fair revenue stream. Furthermore, this allows the company to diversify its portfolio further away from a limited set of blockbuster drugs that have hampered the company's revenue prospects.

Regarding growth, the deal comes at a great moment in time for Pfizer as the drugmaker is facing a significant patent cliff in the coming years. Between 2026 and 2028, Pfizer is set to see several blockbusters, such as Eliquis and Ibrance losing patent protection, which is set to erase up to $18B in annual revenue. This, compounded with the sales of all COVID-19 related solutions falling off a mountain, means Pfizer is set to lose a large portion of its overall revenue. Therefore, the deals come at the right moment in time, as the obesity market is set to grow at a CAGR of over 25% through the 2030s, and Metsera’s pipeline is set to deliver a series of products starting in 2028.

Pfizer also has strong margins, with the company's gross margin exceeding 72%, up from 58.1% in 2023. Furthermore, Pfizer can generate higher net margins than peers such as Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) or GSK plc (GSK). Pfizer is also producing strong free cash flow margins, though it stands at the lower end of its peer group. Without the deal, I was expecting a relatively stable bottom line for the company, as it could offset the patent losses with the rollout of new drug products. This was also coupled with the projected $7.2B in annual cost savings that will come by the end of 2027. With the acquisition of Metsera, Pfizer will need to spend heavily in R&D, as late-stage clinical trials are expensive, and we could see a dip in the company’s operating margins. Until the expected launch towards 2028, this will be a net loss, going directly against Pfizer’s margins. Pfizer also needs to improve its return metrics, as the company currently has a ROE of 12.17% which is at the lower end of the spectrum, as well as a ROIC of 5.6%.

Risks

Pfizer paid a large premium for Metsera, which only recently went public with its IPO earlier in 2025. The entire deal is contingent on the company passing large-scale Phase 3 trials, which is never a guarantee, and if it were to fail, Pfizer could potentially see its investments completely written down. Furthermore, this pushes Pfizer towards a competition-heavy industry with Novo and Eli Lilly already at each other’s throats that are also fighting compounded semaglutide. Though I expect the situation to normalize as the FDA is cracking down on compounded versions, Pfizer is entering the race at an even later stage than Roche with its acquisition of Carmot Therapeutics back in 2023.

Conclusion

With all of that in mind, I still believe that Pfizer is a Strong Buy. The company is still one of the cheapest offerings in the pharmaceutical space, and with its massive capital resources plus manufacturing might, it is only normal that the company is actively diversifying its offerings through M&A. We will have to see how the overall deal pans out, but I see it as a positive.

data.title: Why I Prefer PDO Over PDI
data.description:
PDI and PDO CEF: previous thesis and new developments

I last analyzed the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (NYSE:PDI) fund on 8.8 with an article titled “PDI CEF: Best Time To Sell In 5 Years”. The analysis was triggered by the record-thin credit spread and rated PDI as a sell. As for the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (NYSE:PDO), I last covered it on 7.30 with an article titled “PDO: 11% Yield Is Not Enough.” The analysis benchmarked its yield by risk-free rates and argued for a hold rating.

Since those analyses, there have been a few material developments both in terms of fund-specific changes and macroeconomics. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top developments in each category as I see it. The changes in the first category involve their last premium over NAV (Net Asset Value) and also their latest UNII data (Undistributed Net Investment Income), and the change in the second category involves the interest cut and the updated dot plot announced by the Federal Reserve in mid-September. I will detail why these changes continue to suggest a more attractive return/risk profile for PDO than PDI.

I will start with the price premiums. As seen from the charts provided below, the PDI and PDO funds have historically traded at different valuation premiums relative to their NAV. Historically, PDI has enjoyed a higher premium to its NAV on average. It has had an average premium of 9.26% since 2021 and the premium has been as high as 19.7%. In contrast, PDO’s premium has been much tighter and hovered around an average of just 1.25%. Since my last writing, PDI’s premium has widened considerably and now sits at 16.46%. PDO’s premium expanded too, but to a much lesser extent. As a net result, PDI’s current premium is far above PDO’s 5.14% premium, its own historical average, and among the highest levels observed in the long term.

As will be detailed next, there are certainly good reasons for PDI’s premium expansion. But I believe the expansion is overdone by this point, thus leading me to expect more downside than upside risks going forward.

PDI and PDO CEF: basic information

Before going into further depth, let me cover some basics of both funds for reasons new to them. Both PDI and PDO CEFs are taxable funds managed by PIMCO (Pacific Investment Management Company). Like many other PIMCO funds, both funds focus on current income generation as detailed in their fund description below.

PDI fund description: PDI seeks current income as a primary objective and capital appreciation as a secondary objective. The Fund seeks to achieve its investment objectives by utilizing a dynamic asset allocation strategy among multiple fixed income sectors in the global credit markets, including corporate debt, mortgage-related and other asset-backed securities, government and sovereign debt, taxable municipal bonds and other fixed-, variable- and floating-rate income-producing securities of U.S. and foreign issuers, including emerging market issuers.

PDO fund description below, the fund seeks high income with substantial exposure to high-yield assets in the credit market, with mortgage-related assets as a good example, as detailed next. PDO utilizes an opportunistic approach to pursue high conviction income-generating ideas across credit markets to seek current income as a primary objective and capital appreciation as a secondary objective. The fund will normally invest at least 25% of its total assets in mortgage-related assets issued by government agencies or other governmental entities or by private originators or issuers.

As you can see from the following chart, PDI is a much older fund incepted in 2012, and PDO has a more recent inception in 2021. As such, PDI features a much larger AUM of $6.94 billion compared to PDO’s $1.76 billion. In terms of expense ratios, PDI’s notional ratio is 2.18%, far above PDO’s 1.15%. But as to be detailed next, a better comparison of their fees requires an understanding of their leverage and interest expenses.

PDI and PDO CEF: leverage and interest expenses in focus

To generate high current income, both funds have large exposure to high-yield instruments and also apply considerable leverage. As such, it comes as no surprise that both funds prices and price-to-NAV premium enjoyed a boost since my last writing as the Fed announced an interest cut and an updated dot plot that signals further cuts in the near future.

To wit, per the data as of August 31, PDI's Total Managed Assets are $10,079 million, considerably higher than its Common Net Asset of $6,958 million as seen in the chart below. This is due to various forms of borrowing. Specifically, its use of Reverse Repurchase Agreements is the primary form of leverage, constituting 30.04% of its total managed assets. With some other minor leverage mechanism, the fund's Total Effective Leverage is around 30.96%.

PDO uses leverage even more aggressively, as you can see from the following chart. PDO's Total Managed Assets are $2,681 million. Compared to its Common Net Asset of $1,767 million, this translates into a Total Effective Leverage of around 34.07%. And again, the use of Reverse Repurchase Agreements is the primary form of leverage here too.

With the above analysis of their leverage, we can now revisit their fees and expense ratios. As displayed in the table below, PDI's Total Expense is at 4.46%, notably below PDO’s 5.22%. However, a closer look at the breakdown reveals this is largely due to the Interest Expense. For PDI, the total expense ratio excluding interest expense ratio is 1.67% and 4.46% including interest expense, resulting in an interest expense of 2.79%. In comparison, PDO’s total expense ratio excluding interest expense ratio is 1.84% and 5.22% with interest expense included, resulting in an interest expense of 3.38%.

The difference between the two funds' total expenses is primarily driven by the differences in their leverage ratios and also borrowing rates, which can subsequently translate into different responses to future interest cuts as elaborated on next.

Other risks and final thoughts

As aforementioned, I consider the Fed’s recent decision to lower interest rates and its updated dot plot as a positive for both funds. However, given the above analysis, I expect the boost to PDO to be more pronounced than PDI due to PDO’s higher leverage, higher borrowing rates, and also higher interest expenses.

In particular, I expect lower rates to improve PDO’s UNII (Undistributed Net Investment Income) more than PDI’s. A quick look at past performance indicates that PDO has a higher reliance on a return on capital (ROC) to fund its distributions than PDO. For example, PDI has consistently maintained a rolling coverage ratio below 100% in the past year as seen in the chart below. In other words, it has been distributing less than it earns from its net investment income (NII). Its rolling coverage ratio is 64%, 45%, and 80% for the past three, six, and twelve months.

In contrast, PDO’s rolling coverage ratio has been notably higher and exceeded 100% for certain periods. As shown below, PDO’s rolling coverage ratio is 91%, 55%, and 120% for the past three, six, and twelve months. Given these statistics, I expect a lower-rate environment to reduce PDO’s expenses to a larger degree than PDI and thus offer more upside for PDO’s NII coverage.

In terms of downside risks, PDO’s higher leverage ratio can amplify both gains and losses compared to PDI. My above thesis is based on my expectation for continued rate cuts and lower rates going forward. There is a chance that this expectation does not materialize. In this case, I expect PDO to face stronger headwinds.

Finally, a factor I mentioned above but did not emphasize was the potential valuation impacts from future interest rate changes. Given their emphasis on fixed-income securities, changes in rates could also impact their valuation premiums/discounts to different degrees. But note that these funds have very similar leveraged-adjusted effective duration, indicating similar interest rate sensitivity. More specifically, PDI has a total leveraged-adjusted effective duration of 3.67 years, slightly higher than the PDO’s effective duration of 3.56 years as seen in the chart below.

To close, this reexamination of PDI and PDO is promoted by 3 developments: PDI’s unusual price premium, their latest UNII data, and the updated rate outlook. These developments have led me to conclude that PDI’s current price premium has already factored in the impacts of lower rates. From this point on, I see PDO better positioned to benefit, considering its leverage structure and interest expenses.

data.title: Brookfield Infrastructure Partners: Now Even Better
data.description:
Article Thesis

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (NYSE:BIP) (NYSE:BIPC) is a high-quality dividend growth stock that benefits from macro tailwinds such as declining interest rates and massive Artificial Intelligence investments that are good for BIP's power and electricity assets, as well as for its data center business. Add a nice operational performance and an undemanding valuation, and Brookfield Infrastructure looks like a highly compelling long-term pick, I believe.

Past Coverage

I have written about Brookfield Infrastructure Partners here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in June, around three months ago. I was bullish back then but believe that BIP/BIPC is an even better investment today, thanks to a slightly lower price, the Federal Reserve's interest rate reduction, ongoing massive spending on AI data center infrastructure, and also due to BIP's recently reported compelling Q2 results. With around one quarter having passed since my last article on BIP, it is time to take another look at the company today.

The Connection To Interest Rates

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners isn't a bank, insurance company, or other type of business with a very clear connection to interest rates. Still, interest rate movements impact Brookfield Infrastructure, which is why the company should benefit from declining interest rates. The Federal Reserve has lowered the benchmark rate this September, following a longer pause, and many Federal Reserve board members see further interest rate reductions in the coming quarters. The following chart shows the Federal Reserve's dot plot as of the most recent FED meeting in September:

The chart shows that Federal Reserve members basically all see interest rates declining in 2025, with further declines expected in 2027. Declining interest rates are good for Brookfield Infrastructure Partners in several ways, I believe:

First, lower interest rates make project financing easier, all else equal. When Brookfield Infrastructure acquires or builds out new infrastructure, it usually doesn't finance those with equity only but takes on some debt. Lower interest rates mean cheaper debt, which makes financing projects easier and cheaper, allowing for better profitability.

Second, lower interest rates mean that fixed-income investments become less attractive for major investors. In a low-interest-rate environment, insurers, pension funds, and so on need to seek out higher-yielding investments to boost their returns. This should make them interested in partnering with Brookfield Infrastructure and other real asset investors, which results in more opportunities for BIP, with the same also holding true for other real asset investors such as Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) (BEPC), real estate, and so on.

Third, lower interest rates mean that individual investors will likely be looking for income stocks – bonds become lower-yielding, all else equal, making them less appealing for retirees and other income-focused investors. Brookfield Infrastructure Partners, with its pretty high dividend yield of 5.6%, could be in high demand – income investors could bid up this stock, along with other income stocks such as REITs, if interest rates continue to decline in the coming quarters and years.

All in all, I thus like the macro setup for Brookfield Infrastructure Partners: Interest rates are moving lower again, and at least according to current FED projections, that will continue going forward, which should be pretty good for Brookfield Infrastructure Partners and many other real asset income stocks.

BIP's Data Center Exposure

In recent months, we saw bigger and bigger data center and cloud computing deals, thanks to the ongoing rise of Artificial Intelligence. This includes a gigantic order intake pace for Oracle (ORCL), Nvidia's (NVDA) recent $100 billion deal with OpenAI, and the announcement of Meta Platform's (META) massive Hyperion data center in Louisiana.

All of these deals and the announcements of ongoing huge investments in data centers and AI computing power are good for Brookfield Infrastructure Partners, as the company should benefit in two ways.

First, data centers are extremely power-hungry, meaning demand for electricity, power generation capacity, power transportation infrastructure, energy storage, and so on will continue to grow. Brookfield Infrastructure's utilities segment will benefit from that, as BIP is a major provider of power connections, power transmission (thanks to thousands of kilometers of transmission lines), and so on. More power demand will most likely mean more demand for natural gas, as burning natural gas is a way to generate electric power that does not depend on specific weather conditions. Via its natural gas assets, such as pipelines, storage, etc., BIP should thus benefit from that trend as well.

Second, Brookfield Infrastructure will benefit directly via its data business. BIP owns more than 100 data centers, has tens of thousands of kilometers of fiber optic cable, and so on. With more and more companies wanting to use more and more data, and since all of that data must be transported, BIP's data business should continue to see strong demand and have considerable pricing power. It should also be easy for BIP to work on new projects, as it is a well-capitalized and experienced partner for companies that want to build out their data infrastructure.

While the focus regarding Artificial Intelligence investments is currently clearly on tech stocks, companies like Brookfield Infrastructure should benefit as well – so far, that's not priced into BIP's stock, but that could happen in the coming years.

BIP: Recent Results And Outlook

In summer, Brookfield Infrastructure reported its most recent earnings results. The company showed compelling funds from operations per share growth of 9%, accounting for currency rate movements, which is, I believe, pretty nice for a high-yield company like BIP that isn't valued at a high valuation at all.

Revenue growth was a little lower than that, at 6%, but Brookfield Infrastructure Partners benefitted from operating leverage – costs didn't grow as much as revenue, which is why funds from operations grew a little more than revenue. Over the last five years, Brookfield Infrastructure managed to grow its funds from operations per share by 14% per year on average, which is very compelling, but I believe that growth in the future will likely be a little lower – the starting point is now considerably higher than it was five years ago, making maintaining a pretty high growth rate complicated.

Still, even if BIP only grows its funds from operations per share by 8% to 10% per year going forward, that would still be highly compelling, I believe. Investors get a dividend yield of 5.6% at current prices, so in combination with a high-single-digit funds from operations growth rate, a mid-teens annual return would be possible. Even if BIP were to only grow its funds from operations and its dividend by 4% to 5% per year going forward, annual returns would likely be in the 10% range, assuming BIP's valuation does not move a lot from where it is today. That would be a relatively weak growth rate, considering the past growth and the aforementioned tailwinds from AI, interest rates, and so on.

I thus believe that total returns, including BIP's dividend, should be at least 10% per year over a multi-year time frame, possibly significantly higher if BIP is able to achieve a growth rate of close to 10% and/or if Brookfield Infrastructure Partners' valuation expands from where it is today – BIP trades at just below 10x FFO.

Takeaway

BIP got a little cheaper in recent weeks, despite being a beneficiary of both declining interest rates and the ongoing huge investments in AI, data centers, and so on.

At current prices, the stock is priced for a pretty low growth rate, which seems unrealistic to me. I believe that over a multi-year time frame, returns should be compelling, including thanks to a high starting yield of 5.6%.

data.title: ARKK: Trading Into The Wind
data.description:
Introduction

The ARK Innovation ETF (BATS:ARKK) needs little introduction, although I wrote an educational article on ARKK back in June for anyone who wants the basics. I also wrote the educational articles on Seeking Alpha for ARKF, ARKG, and ARKX too; you can find those on my profile by searching the tickers.

Today, I am giving my annual update on ARKK, since my last opinion article, "The Shotgun Approach Isn't Working," back in May 2024. Since then, it has done very well, especially during the most recent bull run following the April crash.

It's all about timing and perspective, though. That's one of the perks (detractors?) of the shotgun approach.

Sometimes you hit big, sometimes you miss.

Your returns with ARKK will highly depend on when you buy and sell the fund. Remember the long-term trend of this fund, and the past bubble that still haunts it.

My caution around investing in innovation has turned out prudent, not just with our knowledge at the time, but overall. Even buying in at the start of the AI arms race (release of ChatGPT) wasn't enough to outcompete the Nasdaq 100, although how much of this next chart is just measuring how much NVIDIA (NVDA) exposure each fund has. That's even more true for ARK's private market focused mutual fund, the ARK Venture Fund (ARKVX), which underperformed as well.

Trading Into the Wind

If you own or follow ARKK, you should watch this website, which tracks the trades of the various ARK funds, including ARKK. They take ARK's briefings of the trades they do each day and aggregate them, along with the daily holdings accounting, on one webpage.

That's because ARK trades daily, actively raising and lowering their stakes in different stocks across their funds. This is one of the core issues I see for ARK; they trade too much for their own good. I don't have the data, but I would love to see how their past picks from 2021 would've performed today if there was no active trading and moving around.

What prompted me to look at ARK was an interesting thing that rarely happens: new stocks were added to the portfolio. What ARK invests in does change, but most daily traded is them trading shares of stocks they already own. When they add or remove holdings entirely, it's an interesting development. Sometimes, it brings small and mid-cap companies to my attention for further research. Sometimes, it's just another red flag — like this one was.

If you're a regular reader of mine, you may know that I am fairly bearish on Chinese markets. So it was absolutely a red flag to see the newest additions to ARKF and ARKW in the last few days.

Context: the 100% being the "% of position" means that this buy started the position. That is also indicated with the star by the date.

When new stocks trickle into multiple sister funds, it's only a matter of time until they make their way into the flagship. Lo and behold, Cathie is starting to add more Chinese stocks, starting with Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU), a few days later.

Worse, they are selling their holdings in strong firms like AMD (AMD) and speculative bets that have paid off like Tempus AI (TEM).

In fact, they have been selling TEM consistently since their last buy in July. These are just from ARKK and do not include all the selling from ARKG.

I won't get into the specifics of why I don't like Chinese stocks here, but I recommend taking a look at this article from my colleague Julia Ostian on BABA and why it's not a buy after this last run-up.

My ultimate point is that this is another rotation and change in direction for ARKK, and likely a more permanent one given that the stocks are also being bought in funds like ARKF and ARKW. It is a direction I am not fond of, and do not see much promise in, although I was already fairly bearish on ARKK's overall premise of investing in innovation. The "shotgun approach" has to evolve, and now it is evolving to include more Chinese exposure and less US frontier tech mid-caps. That's not a move that inspires confidence in me, and it is not budging my rating on ARKK, regardless of its recent successes. Again, back to timing being everything with this fund, because of its volatility.

Does Innovation Bring Riches?

The second core issue is that technological innovation tends to be deflationary, and as such may not lead to actual, realized market returns. You can change the world, invent a technology that revolutions a field, and fail to deliver on the valuation multiple speculators have bought your company up to. There is a large gap between real-world performance and market returns, and the gap gets wider and wider the smaller the firm — remember that ARK specializes in mid-cap firms, usually, but are trending toward large-caps lately. I worry that investors will not see the kinds of actual returns that bullish forecasters like Cathie Wood, and the ARK Invest team more broadly, make.

Remember the ARK Invest 2020 prediction of Tesla (TSLA) at $6,000 by 2025? I mean, she still has some time, but it's definitely unlikely. While Tesla was still a good investment five-years on, it's nowhere near the level of return that was promised.

I worry investors' FOMO and greed, which make us make poor investment decisions, are triggered by these kinds of projections. They make us eager to jump in too, especially given how charismatic Cathie and her team can be at times on CNBC or their YouTube channel.

Conclusion

Ultimately, my position on ARKK has not changed during this last run-up. They are still a poor way to achieve high-beta investing, and are even poorer as a proxy for a venture capital position. At least the ARK Venture Fund is trying something different with the private equity exposure, but both it and ARKK have underperformed over the long run because of two core issues that they have still not shaken and seem unlikely to shake given the current trends:

  1. They trade too much and are investing in new stocks that I do not care for.
  2. They regularly overestimate the likely returns of the firms they invest in.

For these reasons, ARKK remains a fund that I do not recommend. There are other options, and I think investors wanting exposure to frontier technologies are better served by the ARK sister funds that focus on a specialty or with other frontier-tech funds that take a more passive approach, like QTUM and AGIX.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Amprius Technologies: More Euphoria Needed To Push This Futuristic Battery Maker Higher
data.description:
Amprius Technologies (NYSE:AMPX) is building batteries of the future. They are faster charging, have a greater energy density for longer operational times, and are safer to use. I last covered AMPX in 2023, highlighting the ticker as a possible speculative buy on the back of the latent value of its batteries against multiple billion-dollar total addressable markets, albeit flagging the risk of buying at too high a valuation on what were then still anemic revenues. The Fremont, California-based company has been on a sustained run of purchase order wins since then, ramping up its revenue profile and cementing the product market fit of its batteries. AMPX generated fiscal 2025 second quarter revenue of $15.07 million, up a remarkable 349.9% over its year-ago comp and a beat by $2.65 million on consensus estimates.

AMPX keeps on stacking up purchase orders. The company recently announced a $35 million follow-on order for its SiCore cells from a UAS manufacturer after receiving a $15 million purchase order from the same UAS manufacturer back in February. The order wins, and hyper year-over-year revenue growth figures have sparked euphoria around the common shares, pushing them up 284% year-to-date. This means a market cap of $1.4 billion against trailing 12-month sales of $44.8 million for a 32x sales multiple. Bulls would be right to highlight that revenue growth is ramping upwards fast, and the current pace would almost certainly see this multiple cut in half by next year. AMPX is scaling up and not currently profitable; sales present the most relevant way to assign a valuation to its common shares.

Purchase Orders And Drone Executive Order

Is AMPX still a buy? It depends. Peer speculative futuristic battery company Enovix (ENVX) trades on a much higher $2 billion market cap despite a revenue profile that is materially more constrained than AMPX. ENVX generated revenue of just $7.5 million for its second quarter. While both companies are not explicitly chasing the same end markets, there are overlaps. Hence, on a comparative basis, AMPX somewhat presents better value than ENVX, with a revenue trendline that's ramping up and purchase orders that continue to come in. However, it also opens up sentiment as a critical component of the company's valuation. The market has, to a large extent, valued ENVX higher based on vibes or stronger animal spirits. These have been raging through 2025 on the back of AI hype, eVTOL momentum, and strong defense spending on drones. 2025 for AMPX has seen the company expand its manufacturing capacity through a contract with a South Korean battery manufacturer. Sentiment can ebb and flow, and AMPX could be caught on the other side of this if it switches to become negative.

A wide-ranging drone-focused executive order in June that pushed to fast-track U.S. drone manufacturing operations, cut red tape to make it easier for the military to buy drones, and established an Electric Vertical Takeoff ("eVTOL") pilot program that has also underpinned a ton of the euphoria in the sector. AMPX has built itself a niche in supplying its batteries to the drone industry, with its most recent win a few weeks ago to supply its SiCore cells to power a fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicle being developed by Danish drone manufacturer Nordic Wing. AMPX focuses heavily on applications in the drone industry, with the nascent eVTOL industry and fixed-wing electric aircraft being pursued by management. eVTOL stocks have been on fire this year, with Archer Aviation (ACHR) up over 200% over the last year and Joby Aviation (JOBY) up by a similar amount.

Liquidity And Cash Flow Risks

AMPX is in the process of extending its liquidity position. This was $54.2 million as of the end of the second quarter, against negative cash from operations of $4.3 million. This means a cash runway of just over three years, with the company raising $9.8 million from the sale of common stock during the quarter. AMPX has placed its monthly cash run rate at around $2.5 million to $3 million per month and had $46.7 million left on its at-the-market facility as of the end of June.

Conclusion

AMPX has built itself a position at the intersection of a number of fast-growing markets. The growth in defense spending in response to Russia's invasion of Ukraine, the executive order to boost U.S. drone competitiveness, and market enthusiasm for EVTOLs have all aggregated to significantly ramp up the revenue profile of AMPX. The company valuation at 32x trailing 12-month sales is expensive, but with revenue growing by triple digits year-over-year, it's not hard to see why some investors are willing to pay any price for AMPX common shares. However, the company is currently guiding to burn through cash at a rate of roughly $2.5 million per month and will be dependent on selling shares to bolster a liquidity position that had dropped, over the summer, to its lowest position since the ticker went public. I'm rating AMPX as a hold.

data.title: REITs For A Rate-Cut Rebound: My Top 5 Picks
data.description:
Is Real Estate Ready For A Rebound?

Residential and commercial real estate companies and real estate investment trusts (REITs) could stand to gain from the latest interest rate cutting cycle, which began earlier in September with similar cuts expected for October and February. Lower mortgage rates make houses more affordable and are likely to spur homebuying, while lower borrowing costs could prop up commercial property values, leading to more sales and lending.

In a recent article, I highlighted how the 25 basis point cut may boost some sectors, offering consumers an opportunity to finance at lower rates. The commercial side of real estate may see the benefits of lower rates much more quickly, unlike home mortgages, which do not track the Fed’s moves directly. Much of commercial real estate is financed with shorter-term or floating-rate loans, which are more sensitive to interest rate cuts.

In the wake of the 2022 hikes, commercial property values fell over 20%, sales sank, and banks stopped lending. After the Fed announced the initial cut on September 17, real estate giant CBRE revised its forecast upward, citing lower borrowing costs. CBRE now projects commercial real estate investment volume to increase by about 15% in 2025 - up from its earlier projection of 10%.

Falling short-term rates alone may not be sufficient for a full recovery if the 10Y Treasury yield remains elevated, keeping long-term financing costs high. Add to that risks and uncertainties from tariffs, stubborn inflation, and a softer labor market. That’s why investors should focus on REIT fundamentals - stocks with consistent operating results, reasonable valuations, and reliable dividends.

REITs Primed For A Rate-Cutting Cycle

Although real estate has underperformed the market in the past year, lower interest rates could spur capital inflows. According to CenterSquare investment strategist, Uma Moriarity, institutional investors have been waiting for this type of easing monetary policy environment to deploy assets into real estate. Moriarity also said that, historically, REITs tend to outperform core private real estate once the Fed starts a rate-cutting cycle. In fact, REITs had begun showing momentum ahead of the Fed rate cuts, with the FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs Index rising 3.3% in August.

Health care has been the highest performing REIT sector year-to-date, with a total return of 21.3% – followed by diversified and gaming. Despite a rough market, REIT operating performance in Q2 2025 remained resilient, as they continued to deliver year-over-year growth in net operating income (NOI) - led by industrial REITs (+23.6% YoY).

In addition to gaining a foothold in a sector that could benefit from interest rate easing, REITs can be a defensive haven when the market is volatile, due to their stable dividend income, tangible asset backing, and inflation-hedging properties. With these objectives in mind, SA Quant screened for high-yield REITs with exceptional fundamentals and found five poised to perform in a low-rate environment.

Top 5 REITs

SA Quant identified five Strong Buy commercial REITs that own a wide variety of properties, including hotels, casinos, senior housing, and industrial warehouses. The stocks on the list have outstanding collective investment fundamentals, robust FFO generation, and high earnings growth potential. The stocks also offer safe and high-yielding dividends, providing investors with downside protection in a volatile market.

1. Host Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (HST)

  • Market Capitalization: $12.10B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 4 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 1 out of 15

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

Host Hotels & Resorts, the only S&P 500 lodging REIT, has ridden the post-pandemic leisure and travel rebound to deliver strong growth in revenue, FFO and AFFO - while offering investors stability with a reliable and growing dividend. The top quant-rated Hotel & Resort REIT, HST, has a portfolio of 80 hotels under major brands like Marriott, Hyatt, and the Four Seasons - concentrated in high-demand markets. By consolidating its focus even further on higher-end properties in recent years, Host has benefited from substantial out-of-room revenue from both guests and non-guests.

Higher room rates across the portfolio and greater spending on food, beverage, and services drove Q2 revenue, up 8.2% year-over-year to $1.59B, and helped HST beat earnings for the sixth straight quarter. Healthy transient leisure demand and the continuing recovery in Maui more than offset a decline in group demand, while improvements in room rates offset rising wage expenses. HST lifted full-year EBITDA and revenue guidance, citing outperformance in H125, hotel operational improvements, and expected growth in Maui.

HST’s FFO has surged by a CAGR of ~47% in the past 3 years, driving an A Growth Grade, while exceptional interest and dividend coverage ratios underpin a quality profitability score. HST also offers a stable and rewarding dividend, sporting a yield of 5.18%. Impressive payout ratios support the company’s ‘A’ Dividend Safety Grade while a 3Y CAGR of 115% contributes to a top-notch Dividend Growth Grade. HST’s dividend consistency grade took a hit after the company suspended payouts in mid-2020, preserving cash as it dealt with the fallout from the pandemic. Host’s dividend has grown steadily since it was reinstated in early 2022.

HST is trading at an attractive valuation – with a price-to-FFO of 8.7x – representing a 35% discount to the sector’s 13.4x. HST provides investors with a great opportunity to play the high-end travel boom, backed by premium asset positioning, improving momentum, and a high-quality dividend – all at an attractive price.

2. National Health Investors, Inc. (NHI)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.77B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 7 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 1 out of 17

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Health Care REITs

An aging population and surging demand for senior housing have created powerful tailwinds for National Health Investors, Inc. (NHI), SA Quant’s top-rated Health Care REIT. With senior housing representing two-thirds of NOI, National Health Investors is positioned to benefit from long-term demographic trends. The 85+ population growth rate is expected to accelerate to a CAGR of 1.9% by 2030 and 3.5% in the following decade. On the supply side, inventory growth has dropped below 1% while new starts in Q2 2025 were the lowest level ever recorded – and 64% below the historical average.

Boosted by these dynamics, NHI delivered better than expected Q2 results, with NOI surging nearly 30% to $3.8M, primarily due to increased revenue from higher occupancy levels and revenue per occupied room. Excellent year-over-year growth in its Senior Housing Operating Portfolio, or SHOP, and continued collections on deferral repayments also drove outperformance. Looking ahead, NHI said it has $750M in liquidity and capital resources to continue executing on a $350M pipeline - which is nearly entirely focused on senior housing.

Improving momentum in the past 3 months, along with rising earnings and upward EPS revisions, has bolstered NHI’s Strong Buy Quant Rating. Robust AFFO is driving NHI’s ‘A’ Profitability Grade. NHI’s FFO growth has improved in the last 12 months and is projected to rise steadily in each of the next three years, according to consensus estimates.

NHI offers a safe and consistent dividend, yielding 4.63%, with 33 consecutive annual payouts. Growth has lagged, but NHI increased its dividend for the first time in four years - a payout of $0.92 per share will be distributed in Q3. NHI is trading at a premium to the sector with a P/FFO of 16.50x – but positive tailwinds, a safe dividend, and stellar margins, still make it a Strong Buy stock.

3. Xenia Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (XHR)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.46B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 8 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 2 out of 15

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

XHR has crushed the real estate sector in the past three months, up more than 15%, boosted by quality financial results across its 30-hotel portfolio and three demand segments, which include Group (~35% of revenue), Corporate Transient (~40%), and Leisure Transient (25%). XHR’s Q2 revenue of $287.58M (+5.4% YoY), beat expectations by $14.23M, and adjusted FFO per share rose 9.6%, resulting from strong group business demand, a recovery in corporate transient, and normalization in leisure.

Xenia’s Q2 performance was driven by sturdy revenue from the recently renovated Grand Hyatt Scottsdale Resort, substantial food and beverage sales portfolio-wide, and outsized gains in its highly-profitable catering business. XHR has delivered amazing FFO growth in the past three years, up by a CAGR of 78% to outperform the sector. And XHR’s outlook is impressive, with Wall Street analysts projecting earnings to grow by 9% in FY25.

Strong reinvestment and FFO dividend coverage ratios, are driving a solid profitably grade. Trading at 8.3x FFO, XHR is at a significant discount to the sector for a ‘B’ Valuation Grade. XHR offers a safe dividend, yielding 3.89%, that has delivered long-term growth. With an attractive price, outstanding growth, and accelerating momentum, XHR is another Strong Buy hotel REIT for your portfolio.

4. STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.52B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 10 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 1 out of 13

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Industrial REITS

#1 among quant-rated Industrial REITs, STAG boasts 600 properties in 41 states - totaling 118.3M square feet - and has seen positive momentum after it beat earnings for the eighth straight quarter in Q2. STAG has delivered substantial growth and profitability in the past year as conditions improve in the medium-sized warehouse market.

STAG has already leased 90.8% of the square feet it expected to lease in 2025, while achieving cash leasing spreads of 24.5% in the second quarter. STAG has seen a recovery in demand and tightening supply as trade tensions dissipate and businesses look to diversify supply chains, which CEO William Cooker elaborated on in the earnings call:

In the first quarter, news related to the global trade war drove significant market volatility. Broadly speaking, today, the theme has shifted to a general desensitization to tariff headlines. We are witnessing businesses continue to grow and make corporate decisions in an uncertain environment, a change to the broad pause seen in the past 12 months. While it's certainly not business as usual, users cannot delay space decisions in perpetuity, and supply chain diversification remains a priority for many companies.”

STAG has posted steady growth in operating revenue and return on equity, showcases impressive profit margins, and offers a safe and consistent monthly dividend with a yield of 4.30%. The stock is trading in line with the market with a 13.81x P/FFO ratio, but improving earnings growth projections and strong all-around investment fundamentals offer great exposure to a recovering industrial REIT market.

5. VICI Properties Inc. (VICI)

  • Market Capitalization: $34.01B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 11 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 1 out of 12

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Other Specialized REITs

VICI’s stock slid after Las Vegas reported a sharp drop in visitors in July, creating a potential buy-the-dip opportunity. VICI generates significant revenue from the Las Vegas strip – where it owns three iconic hotels – Caesars Palace, MGM Grand, and The Venetian. However, VICI also derives revenue from about 90 other experiential assets in the US and Canada. VICI is now trading at a 12% discount to the sector, at 11.7x FFO, which is attractive given the stock’s exceptional collective investment qualities, high earnings growth upside, and sturdy dividend yield.

Moreover, the rate cut could benefit VICI by lowering refinancing costs, making it even more of a value play, according to SA Analyst Steven Fiorillo:

They have world-class assets that could increase in value as property values increase and rates decline. VICI could also further reduce its carrying costs by refinancing additional debt at lower rates and improving its margins as less capital flows toward interest expenses… As the year progresses, I think that shares of VICI can continue to move higher while rewarding investors with a dividend yield that exceeds risk-free assets.

VICI’s FFO margin of nearly 70% is helping fuel its A+ Profitability Grade, while year-over-year and long-term growth in AFFO and FFO are crushing the sector. VICI’s Dividend Scorecard is solid across the board - with B’s in safety, growth, yield and consistency. Showcasing a yield of 5.64%, VICI’s dividend has grown by a CAGR of 7% in the past five years. The gaming REIT has delivered six consecutive years of dividend payments and growth since going public in 2018.

VICI is the final pick on our diverse list of Strong Buy REITs - a great stable of stocks that possess excellent underlying investment fundamentals, dividends you can depend on, and exceptional earnings growth upside relative to the real estate sector.

Conclusion: Top 5 REITs Poised For A Rate-Cut Rebound

The real estate sector stands to gain from interest rate cuts, with lower mortgage rates likely to stimulate homebuying, as lower borrowing costs stabilize commercial property value. REITs tend to perform well during interest-rate cutting cycles, and experts say institutional investors have been waiting for this type of monetary policy environment before deploying assets into real estate.
